target: SFOS 22.0.0.411 cish (Cyberoam Interactive Shell) -- shell escape analysis
description: Complete reverse engineering of the cish restricted shell for command injection and shell escape vulnerabilities

architecture.grammar_format: Custom: #define macros for command templates, %section% blocks for grammar rules, <type> tokens for user input
architecture.pipeline_support: safe_system splits on | to create pipelines of up to 32 commands

findings.title: cish Restricted Shell -- Well-Defended Against Direct Shell Escape
findings.description: The cish restricted shell uses a defense-in-depth architecture that effectively prevents direct command injection: (1) Grammar-constrained input -- users can only fill typed parameter slots, not arbitrary commands; (2) Input filtering -- ; & | blocked in <string>/<text> types; (3) execve()-based execution -- shell metacharacters are not interpreted because commands are NOT passed through /bin/sh -c. The combination makes direct shell escape from cish effectively impossible via the CLI interface alone.
findings.evidence: Binary analysis: safeforkexec() at fcn.08055562 uses fork()+execve() with constructed argv[], not system(). Input filter: strpbrk(input, ';&|') in type validation at fcn.080510b3.
findings.impact: Positive finding. The cish design is sound for a restricted shell.
findings.notes: However, several defense-in-depth gaps exist (see SFOS-021, SFOS-022).

findings.title: cish <buffer> Type Bypasses Metacharacter Filter
findings.description: The <buffer> input type in cish performs quote matching only, without the strpbrk() check that blocks ; & | in <string> and <text> types. The <buffer> type is used in ip_addr.conf and ip6_addr.conf for PATTERN parameters in 'ip addr show' commands. While the execve() execution model prevents shell interpretation, this inconsistency in input validation is a defense-in-depth gap.
findings.evidence: cish binary: <buffer> type handler at does not call strpbrk(). Used in: ip_addr.conf (ip addr show PATTERN), ip6_addr.conf.
findings.impact: Minimal in current code -- execve() prevents shell interpretation regardless. But if the execution model ever changes (e.g., to system()), this becomes exploitable.

findings.title: Shell Scripts Invoked by cish Use Unquoted Variable Expansion
findings.category: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in OS Command (defense-in-depth gap)
findings.description: Several shell scripts invoked by cish use unquoted variable expansion ($*, $1, $arg) which, in a /bin/sh context, undergoes word splitting and globbing. While cish's execve() model delivers arguments safely to the script, and the scripts receive them as individual argv elements, the unquoted $* in constructs like 'conntrack $*' creates a theoretical second-order injection risk if an argument contains IFS characters or glob patterns.
findings.evidence: dgutils.sh line 6: 'conntrack $* 2>/dev/null' (unquoted $*). rlookup.sh line 9: '/bin/ip ro get $arg' (unquoted $arg). telnet.sh line 15: 'exec /bin/telnet $IP $PORT' (unquoted vars, but IP is inet_pton validated by cish).
findings.impact: Minimal in practice. Arguments from cish are already sanitized (no ; & |) and individually tokenized. Glob expansion (* ? [ ]) of argument values is the only remaining risk, and is extremely unlikely to produce useful exploitation. However, these scripts should use quoted "$@" instead of $* for robustness.

findings.title: cish AT Command Injection via WWAN Modem Interface
findings.category: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements
findings.description: The cish command 'system wwan query ATcommand <string>' passes a user-supplied string as an AT command to a cellular modem via /bin/opcode apiInterface. While the <string> type blocks ; & |, AT command syntax uses different control characters. An attacker with admin CLI access could potentially: (1) reconfigure modem APN settings to route traffic through attacker-controlled infrastructure, (2) read SIM card data (ICCID, IMSI), (3) send SMS messages, (4) disable modem security features. Requires a Sophos appliance with cellular/WWAN module.
findings.evidence: set.conf: 'system wwan query ATcommand <string>' maps to '/bin/opcode apiInterface -s sync -t json -b JSON mode 1265 ATcommand <string>'
findings.impact: Modem-level compromise on appliances with WWAN. Not a shell escape, but a lateral vector from admin CLI to cellular modem control plane. Only applicable to appliances with cellular connectivity (XGS models with SIM slots).

negative_findings: No 'console', 'advanced-shell', or 'debug shell' command accessible from cish grammar
No file path parameters (<filename> or <path> types) -- all paths are hardcoded in #define macros
No output redirection (> or >>) available to users -- safe_system splits on | for pipelines but does not support redirection
enableremote command in failsafe.conf uses type-validated <ipaddress> and <number> -- no injection possible
All SQL-touching operations go through /bin/opcode apiInterface which uses the CSC client protocol, not direct SQL
privesc_assessment: cish itself does not provide a path to shell escape or privilege escalation. The restricted shell is the privilege boundary, and it holds. Privesc from an admin cish session would require: (1) exploiting a vulnerability in one of the target binaries cish invokes (conntrack, ip, tcpdump, nslookup, opcode), (2) exploiting the web console's TelnetServlet which provides a cish-like interface but may have different restrictions, or (3) exploiting the Jetty 'Advanced Shell' referenced in documentation but not accessible from cish.
next_steps: Analyze /bin/opcode and the apiInterface protocol for command injection in user-controlled trailing arguments
Check if the web console's TelnetServlet maps to unrestricted cish or a different shell binary
Search for 'Advanced Shell' mechanism in SFOS (web UI path, not cish CLI)
Analyze conntrack, tcpdump, ip binaries for argument-based exploits
